rows.v: Hello / Hi
OH-lah
Hola, ¿cómo estás? — Hello, how are you?
An interjection — no gender or conjugation. The 'h' is always silent in Spanish.
The all-purpose greeting, fine at any time of day and in any setting, formal or casual.
Good morning
BWEH-nohs DEE-ahs
Buenos días, señora. — Good morning, ma'am.
Literally 'good days' — plural by tradition. Note the masculine plural ending '-os'.
Used until around noon. Spanish often greets with the plural form ('días', 'tardes'), which surprises beginners.
Good afternoon
BWEH-nahs TAR-dehs
Buenas tardes a todos. — Good afternoon, everyone.
Feminine plural ('-as') because 'tardes' is feminine — note it differs from 'buenos días'.
Used from about noon until sunset. The shift from 'días' (masc.) to 'tardes' (fem.) trips up many learners.
Good evening / Good night
BWEH-nahs NOH-chehs
Buenas noches, hasta mañana. — Good night, see you tomorrow.
Feminine plural. Serves as both a greeting (good evening) and a farewell (good night).
Unlike English, this one word covers both arriving in the evening and leaving for bed.
Goodbye
ah-DYOHS
Adiós, nos vemos. — Goodbye, see you.
An interjection. Originally 'a Dios' — 'to God' — a blessing for the parting traveler.
Slightly more final than 'hasta luego'; for a quick 'see you soon', Spanish speakers often prefer the latter.
See you later
AHS-tah LWEH-goh
Hasta luego, amigo. — See you later, friend.
'Hasta' = until; 'luego' = later/then. Literally 'until later'.
The everyday casual goodbye, far more common than 'adiós' for ordinary partings.
How are you?
KOH-moh ehs-TAHS
¿Cómo estás hoy? — How are you today?
Uses 'estar' (temporary states), not 'ser'. Informal 'tú' form; the formal version is '¿Cómo está?'.
Note the opening upside-down question mark '¿' — Spanish brackets every question this way.
Very well / Very good
mooy BYEHN
Estoy muy bien, gracias. — I'm very well, thanks.
'Muy' (very) + 'bien' (well). 'Muy' modifies adjectives and adverbs and never changes form.
The standard upbeat reply to '¿cómo estás?'. 'Bien' alone ('fine') is also perfectly natural.
Nice to meet you
MOO-choh GOOS-toh
Mucho gusto, soy Ana. — Nice to meet you, I'm Ana.
Literally 'much pleasure'. A fixed phrase — don't translate it word for word.
Said when meeting someone for the first time. A common reply is 'igualmente' (likewise).
What's your name?
KOH-moh teh YAH-mahs
¿Cómo te llamas? — Me llamo Juan. — What's your name? — My name is Juan.
Literally 'how do you call yourself?' — uses the reflexive verb 'llamarse'. The double 'll' sounds like English 'y'.
Answer with 'Me llamo…' (I call myself…). The formal version is '¿Cómo se llama?'.
Yes
SEE
Sí, por favor. — Yes, please.
Note the accent: 'sí' (yes) vs 'si' (if) — the accent mark changes the meaning entirely.
One of the first words to master, and a clear example of how a Spanish accent mark can flip meaning.
No
NOH
No, gracias. — No, thank you.
Also the all-purpose negator: place it before a verb to make it negative ('no hablo' = I don't speak).
Spanish doesn't need a helper verb like English 'don't' — just put 'no' in front of the verb.
Please
pohr fah-VOHR
Un café, por favor. — A coffee, please.
Literally 'for favor / as a favor'. A fixed phrase, unchanging.
Politeness essential. Comes at the start or end of a request, just like English 'please'.
Thank you
GRAH-syahs
Muchas gracias. — Thank you very much.
Always plural in form. Strengthen it with 'muchas gracias' (many thanks).
The single most useful courtesy word. 'Muchísimas gracias' is an even warmer 'thanks so much'.
You're welcome
deh NAH-dah
— Gracias. — De nada. — Thanks. — You're welcome.
Literally 'of nothing' — equivalent to 'it's nothing'. A fixed reply.
The standard response to 'gracias'. In some regions you'll also hear 'con gusto' or 'a ti'.
Excuse me / Sorry
pehr-DOHN
Perdón, ¿dónde está el baño? — Excuse me, where's the bathroom?
From 'perdonar' (to pardon). Works both to get attention and to apologize.
Use it to squeeze past someone or to begin a question to a stranger — like English 'excuse me'.
I'm sorry
loh SYEHN-toh
Lo siento mucho. — I'm very sorry.
Literally 'I feel it' — from 'sentir' (to feel). Reserved for genuine apology or sympathy.
Deeper than 'perdón': use 'lo siento' for real regret or condolences, 'perdón' for minor bumps.
I don't understand
noh ehn-TYEHN-doh
Perdón, no entiendo. — Sorry, I don't understand.
From 'entender' (to understand), an irregular stem-changing verb (e→ie).
A survival phrase. Pair it with '¿puede repetir?' (can you repeat?) to keep a conversation going.
Do you speak English?
AH-blah een-GLEHS
¿Habla inglés? — Do you speak English?
Formal 'usted' form of 'hablar'. Informal would be '¿Hablas inglés?'.
Note 'inglés' is lowercase — Spanish doesn't capitalize languages or nationalities.
One
OO-noh
Uno, dos, tres. — One, two, three.
Becomes 'un' before a masculine noun and 'una' before a feminine one ('un libro', 'una casa').
The number one agrees with gender, unlike the others. 'Un' also serves as 'a/an'.
Two
DOHS
Dos cafés, por favor. — Two coffees, please.
Invariable — doesn't change for gender. Same form everywhere.
From two onward, Spanish numbers don't change form for gender (except hundreds).
Three
TREHS
Son las tres. — It's three o'clock.
Invariable. Note it has no accent, but 'treinta' (thirty) and others do.
Used for counting and telling time. 'Las tres' = three o'clock.
Ten
DYEHS
Tengo diez euros. — I have ten euros.
Invariable. The 'z' is pronounced like 's' in Latin America, like 'th' in much of Spain.
A milestone number; from here you build 'once, doce…' up to twenty.
One hundred
SYEHN
Cien personas. — A hundred people.
'Cien' before a noun, but 'ciento' in compounds ('ciento uno' = 101).
Exactly 100 is 'cien'; 101 onward switches to 'ciento'. A classic beginner gotcha.
What time is it?
keh OH-rah ehs
¿Qué hora es? — Son las dos. — What time is it? — It's two.
Literally 'what hour is it?'. Answer with 'Es la una' (1:00) or 'Son las…' (other hours).
Note: 'es la una' is singular for one o'clock, but 'son las' (plural) for every other hour.
Today
OY
Hoy es lunes. — Today is Monday.
An adverb of time — invariable. Rhymes with English 'boy'.
Pairs with 'ayer' (yesterday) and 'mañana' (tomorrow) — the core time-frame trio.
Tomorrow / Morning
mah-NYAH-nah
Hasta mañana. — See you tomorrow.
'Mañana' means both 'tomorrow' and 'morning' — context decides. The 'ñ' sounds like 'ny' in canyon.
'la mañana' = the morning; 'mañana' alone = tomorrow. One word, two everyday meanings.
Now
ah-OH-rah
Ahora no, gracias. — Not now, thanks.
An adverb. 'Ahora mismo' means 'right now', adding urgency.
Don't confuse with 'ahorita' (Latin American 'in a little while'), which can mean anything from now to later.
Water
AH-gwah
Un vaso de agua, por favor. — A glass of water, please.
Feminine, but takes 'el' in the singular ('el agua') to avoid two a-sounds clashing. Still feminine: 'el agua fría'.
A famous quirk: 'el agua' looks masculine but is feminine, so adjectives stay feminine ('fría', not 'frío').
Coffee
kah-FEH
Un café con leche. — A coffee with milk.
Masculine: 'el café'. Also means 'café' (the place) and the color brown.
'Café con leche' (with milk) and 'café solo' (espresso) are the two you'll order most.
Bread
PAHN
Pan, por favor. — Bread, please.
Masculine: 'el pan'. A short, high-frequency word.
A staple at every meal. 'Pan tostado' is toast; a 'panadería' is the bakery.
The bill / check
lah KWEHN-tah
La cuenta, por favor. — The check, please.
Feminine: 'la cuenta'. From 'contar' (to count).
Essential at restaurants — in Spain and Latin America the bill rarely comes until you ask.
Beer
sehr-VEH-sah
Una cerveza fría. — A cold beer.
Feminine: 'la cerveza'. The 'v' is pronounced much like a soft 'b'.
'Una caña' in Spain is a small draft beer; ordering by size and type varies widely by country.
Food / Meal
koh-MEE-dah
La comida está deliciosa. — The food is delicious.
Feminine: 'la comida'. From 'comer' (to eat). In Spain it often specifically means lunch.
Context matters: 'la comida' can mean food in general, a meal, or specifically the midday meal.
I want / I'd like
KYEH-roh
Quiero un café. — I'd like a coffee.
From 'querer' (to want), an irregular e→ie verb. 'Quiero' = I want.
Softer and more polite: 'quisiera' ('I would like'). 'Quiero' is direct but perfectly acceptable when ordering.
The menu
lah KAR-tah
¿Me trae la carta? — Could you bring me the menu?
Feminine: 'la carta'. Note: 'el menú' usually means a fixed-price set meal, not the menu of dishes.
False friend alert: ask for 'la carta' to see all dishes; 'el menú' often gets you the daily set menu.
Cheers! / Bless you!
sah-LOOD
¡Salud! — Cheers!
Literally 'health'. A noun used as a toast. The 'd' at the end is barely pronounced.
Doubles as 'bless you!' after a sneeze. Toasting, it's the equivalent of 'cheers'.
Where is…?
DOHN-deh ehs-TAH
¿Dónde está la estación? — Where is the station?
'Dónde' (with accent) for questions; uses 'estar' for location. Plural: '¿Dónde están…?'.
The single most useful travel question. 'Está' (location) uses 'estar', never 'ser'.
Bathroom / Restroom
BAH-nyoh
¿Dónde está el baño? — Where's the bathroom?
Masculine: 'el baño'. The 'ñ' gives the 'ny' sound. Also means 'bath'.
In Latin America you may also hear 'el servicio' or 'el sanitario'; in public places, 'los aseos' in Spain.
Left
ees-KYEHR-dah
Gire a la izquierda. — Turn left.
Feminine noun used with 'a la izquierda' (to the left). One of Spanish's trickier words to pronounce.
Direction-giving essential. Pairs with 'a la derecha' (to the right) and 'todo recto' (straight ahead).
Right
deh-REH-chah
A la derecha, por favor. — To the right, please.
Feminine: 'la derecha'. Don't confuse with 'derecho' (straight ahead / law / right as in 'a right').
Tricky pair: 'la derecha' = the right side; 'derecho' (masc.) = straight ahead. One letter, big difference.
Here
ah-KEE
Estoy aquí. — I'm here.
Adverb of place. 'Aquí' (here) vs 'allí' (there) vs 'ahí' (right there).
Spanish has a three-way here/there system; 'aquí' is closest to the speaker.
There
ah-YEE
Está allí. — It's over there.
Adverb of place — farther than 'ahí'. The 'll' sounds like 'y'.
Part of the aquí / ahí / allí trio marking near, mid, and far distance from the speaker.
Station
ehs-tah-SYOHN
La estación de tren. — The train station.
Feminine: 'la estación'. Words ending in '-ción' are feminine and become '-ciones' in plural.
Reliable rule: nearly all '-ción' words are feminine (la nación, la información) — and they drop the accent in the plural.
How much does it cost?
KWAHN-toh KWEHS-tah
¿Cuánto cuesta? — How much is it?
From 'costar' (to cost), an o→ue stem-changing verb. 'Cuánto' takes an accent in questions.
Shopping survival phrase. For multiple items: '¿Cuánto cuestan?' (plural).
I
YOH
Yo soy estudiante. — I am a student.
Subject pronoun. Often dropped, since the verb ending already shows 'I' ('soy' = I am).
Spanish frequently omits subject pronouns — 'soy' alone means 'I am'. Use 'yo' for emphasis or contrast.
You (informal)
TOO
¿Tú hablas español? — Do you speak Spanish?
Informal singular 'you'. Accent matters: 'tú' (you) vs 'tu' (your).
Use 'tú' with friends, family, peers, and children. For respect or distance, use 'usted'.
He / She
EHL / EH-yah
Él es alto. Ella es alta. — He is tall. She is tall.
'Él' (he, with accent) vs 'el' (the). 'Ella' = she. Adjectives agree: 'alto' / 'alta'.
Notice the adjective changes ending to match gender — a core habit to build early.
We
noh-SOH-trohs
Nosotros somos amigos. — We are friends.
Masculine/mixed group: 'nosotros'; all-female group: 'nosotras'.
A mixed-gender group defaults to the masculine form — true of nearly all Spanish plurals.
Friend
ah-MEE-goh
Es mi mejor amigo. — He's my best friend.
'el amigo' (male friend), 'la amiga' (female friend). Gender follows the person.
Many people-nouns swap '-o'/'-a' for male/female. 'Amigos' can mean an all-male or mixed group of friends.
Family
fah-MEE-lyah
Mi familia es grande. — My family is big.
Feminine: 'la familia'. Singular even though it refers to many people.
A collective noun treated as singular: 'la familia es…', not 'son'.
Mother
MAH-dreh
Mi madre se llama Rosa. — My mother's name is Rosa.
Feminine: 'la madre'. Affectionate forms: 'mamá', 'mami'.
'Madre' is the standard word; 'mamá' is the warmer everyday term, like 'mom'.
Father
PAH-dreh
Mi padre trabaja mucho. — My father works a lot.
Masculine: 'el padre'. Plural 'los padres' means 'parents' (mother and father together).
Useful quirk: 'los padres' = 'the parents', not 'the fathers' — the masculine plural covers both.
Man / Woman
OHM-breh / moo-HEHR
El hombre y la mujer. — The man and the woman.
'el hombre' (man, masc.), 'la mujer' (woman, fem.). The 'j' in 'mujer' is a throaty 'h' sound.
Two of the most common nouns, and a clean illustration of natural gender matching grammatical gender.
To be (permanent)
SEHR
Soy de España. — I'm from Spain.
Irregular. Used for identity, origin, time, and lasting traits. Conjugates: soy, eres, es, somos, son.
Spanish has two verbs for 'to be'. 'Ser' is for who/what something fundamentally is — origin, profession, character.
To be (temporary)
ehs-TAR
Estoy cansado. — I'm tired.
Irregular. Used for location, moods, and changing states. Conjugates: estoy, estás, está, estamos, están.
The other 'to be'. 'Estar' covers location and temporary conditions. 'Estoy bien' = I'm (feeling) well.
To have
teh-NEHR
Tengo dos hermanos. — I have two siblings.
Irregular (tengo, tienes, tiene…). Also expresses age: 'tengo 20 años' (I'm 20).
Spanish uses 'tener' (to have) for age, hunger, and thirst: 'tengo hambre' literally = 'I have hunger'.
To go
EER
Voy a casa. — I'm going home.
Highly irregular (voy, vas, va, vamos, van). 'Ir a' + verb expresses the near future.
'Voy a comer' = 'I'm going to eat' — the easiest way to talk about the future before learning the future tense.
To speak / talk
ah-BLAR
Hablo un poco de español. — I speak a little Spanish.
Regular '-ar' verb — the model for a huge group. Conjugates: hablo, hablas, habla, hablamos, hablan.
Learn 'hablar' and you've learned the pattern for thousands of regular '-ar' verbs.
To eat
koh-MEHR
¿Quieres comer algo? — Do you want to eat something?
Regular '-er' verb — the model for that group. Conjugates: como, comes, come, comemos, comen.
The template for regular '-er' verbs. Note 'como' also means 'how/as' — context separates them.
To want / love
keh-REHR
Te quiero. — I love you.
Irregular e→ie (quiero, quieres, quiere…). Means both 'to want' and, with people, 'to love'.
'Te quiero' is the everyday 'I love you' for family and partners; 'te amo' is more intense and romantic.
Can / to be able to
poh-DEHR
¿Puedes ayudarme? — Can you help me?
Irregular o→ue (puedo, puedes, puede…). Followed by another verb in the infinitive.
Essential for polite requests: '¿Puede…?' (formal) and '¿Puedes…?' (informal) both mean 'Can you…?'.
To do / make
ah-SEHR
¿Qué haces? — What are you doing?
Irregular (hago, haces, hace…). Covers both 'do' and 'make'. Also used for weather: 'hace calor' (it's hot).
One verb for both 'do' and 'make'. It also drives weather expressions: 'hace frío' = it's cold.
No problem
noh eye proh-BLEH-mah
— Gracias. — No hay problema. — Thanks. — No problem.
'Hay' = there is/are (from 'haber'). Literally 'there is no problem'.
'Hay' is one of the handiest words in Spanish: 'there is' and 'there are' in a single form.
Of course / Sure
KLAH-roh
— ¿Vienes? — ¡Claro! — Are you coming? — Of course!
An adverb/interjection here. Also an adjective meaning 'clear' or 'light' (color).
'Claro' or 'claro que sí' is an enthusiastic 'of course!'. Extremely common in conversation.
OK / Alright (Spain)
VAH-leh
— Nos vemos a las dos. — Vale. — See you at two. — OK.
From 'valer' (to be worth). In Spain it's the all-purpose 'OK'; less common in Latin America.
A regional marker: 'vale' instantly signals Spain. In Latin America you'll hear 'okay', 'bueno', or 'dale'.
How's it going?
keh TAHL
¿Qué tal? — How's it going?
Literally 'what such?'. A fixed, casual greeting — no verb needed.
More casual than '¿cómo estás?'. Can also mean 'how about…?' ('¿Qué tal el hotel?' = How's the hotel?).
I'm hungry
TEHN-goh AHM-breh
Tengo hambre. ¿Comemos? — I'm hungry. Shall we eat?
Uses 'tener' (to have) + 'hambre' (hunger) — literally 'I have hunger', not 'I am hungry'.
A key difference from English: hunger, thirst, and age use 'tener'. The 'h' in 'hambre' is silent.
I'm lost
ehs-TOY pehr-DEE-doh
Perdón, estoy perdido. — Sorry, I'm lost.
Uses 'estar' (temporary state) + 'perdido/a'. Women say 'perdida'.
Adjective agreement in action: a man says 'perdido', a woman 'perdida'. A vital travel phrase.
I like (it)
meh GOOS-tah
Me gusta el café. — I like coffee.
Literally 'it pleases me' — from 'gustar', which works backwards from English. Plural things: 'me gustan'.
'Gustar' flips the logic: the thing liked is the subject. 'Me gustan los gatos' = 'cats please me' = I like cats.
Enjoy your meal
bwehn proh-VEH-choh
¡Buen provecho! — Enjoy your meal!
A fixed phrase, the Spanish equivalent of 'bon appétit'. 'Buen' is the shortened form of 'bueno' before a noun.
Said before eating, or politely to others who are eating as you pass. Universally warm and common.
